coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional poly(lactic acid) membrane assisted by coal-based carbon dots for efficient separation of oil-in-water emulsions and dyes

coredata.dc:description: Superhydrophilic separation membranes have broad application prospects in oil-water separation and wastewater purification. However, the accumulation of pollutants on the membrane surface and the secondary environmental pollution caused by waste membranes remain inevitable challenges. In this study, a superhydrophilic self-cleaning multifunctional membrane was fabricated by hydrolytic co-deposition of carbon dots, tetrabutyl titanate (TBT), and tannic acid on the surface of a degradable poly(lactic acid) (PLA) membrane for efficient separation of dye/oil/water emulsions. The results indicate that the superhydrophilic crosslinking network is formed on the surface of PLA-based membranes through co-deposition of TA-based coating, enabling the multifunctional membrane to possess a stable and ultra-strong oleophobic hydrophilic layer. As a result, the membrane exhibits strong underwater oil resistance and excellent performance in the separation of oil-in-water emulsions (rejection rate > 99%). More importantly, the surface of the superhydrophilic crosslinking network is negatively charged, which facilitates the selective removal of positively charged organic soluble substances in water through electrostatic adsorption. For instance, the removal rate of cationic dye MB and amphoteric dye RhB can reach as high as 99.93%. Additionally, with the catalysis of TiO2, the organic pollutants on the membrane surface can be decomposed under UV irradiation, indicating the ideal self-cleaning property of the multifunctional membrane. This novel strategy for constructing a multifunctional surface deposition layer is expected to provide broader prospects for the application of superhydrophilic membranes in oil-water separation and wastewater purification.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional poly(lactic acid) membrane assisted by coal-based carbon dots for efficient separation of oil-in-water emulsions and dyes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superhydrophilic separation membranes have broad application prospects in oil-water separation and wastewater purification. However, the accumulation of pollutants on the membrane surface and the secondary environmental pollution caused by waste membranes remain inevitable challenges. In this study, a superhydrophilic self-cleaning multifunctional membrane was fabricated by hydrolytic co-deposition of carbon dots, tetrabutyl titanate (TBT), and tannic acid on the surface of a degradable poly(lactic acid) (PLA) membrane for efficient separation of dye/oil/water emulsions. The results indicate that the superhydrophilic crosslinking network is formed on the surface of PLA-based membranes through co-deposition of TA-based coating, enabling the multifunctional membrane to possess a stable and ultra-strong oleophobic hydrophilic layer. As a result, the membrane exhibits strong underwater oil resistance and excellent performance in the separation of oil-in-water emulsions (rejection rate > 99%). More importantly, the surface of the superhydrophilic crosslinking network is negatively charged, which facilitates the selective removal of positively charged organic soluble substances in water through electrostatic adsorption. For instance, the removal rate of cationic dye MB and amphoteric dye RhB can reach as high as 99.93%. Additionally, with the catalysis of TiO2, the organic pollutants on the membrane surface can be decomposed under UV irradiation, indicating the ideal self-cleaning property of the multifunctional membrane. This novel strategy for constructing a multifunctional surface deposition layer is expected to provide broader prospects for the application of superhydrophilic membranes in oil-water separation and wastewater purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of global industrialization, water pollution has emerged as a problem that cannot be ignored. In particular, the presence of insoluble oils and soluble organic dyes in wastewater poses a serious threat to both the ecological environment and human health [1–4]. Therefore, the integrated treatment technology for oily wastewater containing various pollutants is a current research focus in the field of wastewater purification [5,6]. Common techniques for wastewater treatment include distillation [3], adsorption [7], and membrane separation [8]. Among them, membrane separation technology has attracted wide attention due to its advantages of high efficiency, low cost, and low energy consumption [9,10].

Polymer membranes, such as polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and polyacrylonitrile (PAN), are prone to membrane fouling caused by organic substances due to their inherent hydrophobicity and lipophilicity, which limits their practical application in complex wastewater systems [11,12]. Hydrophilic modification of polymer membranes is an effective means to overcome membrane fouling [13]. Zhou et al. [14] decorated halloysite nanotubes (HNT) on the surface of a PVDF membrane by a one-pot method involving KH550 and dopamine to obtain superhydrophilic and underwater superoleophobic membranes, which could be applied to the separation of oil/water (O/W) emulsions as well as the removal of different water-soluble dyes. A biomimetic underwater superoleophobic membrane was constructed by layer-by-layer deposition of naturally obtained κ-carrageenan (CGN) and a nanoclay laponite onto hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile (h-PAN) membranes [11]. This self-assembled membrane not only exhibits a high separation efficiency of 99.3% for O/W emulsions, but also effectively removes soluble dyes and metal ions from water by electrostatic adsorption. Wang et al. [15] fabricated negatively charged superhydrophilic membranes with excellent oil-water separation capability and superior dye degradation ability by depositing β-FeOOH nanorods on commercially available sulfonated polyolefin lithium-ion battery separators (LIBS). These studies mentioned above have demonstrated that the effective superhydrophilic modification of polymer membranes can significantly enhance the anti-pollution performance of the membranes and also enable the comprehensive treatment of oil-soluble and water-soluble pollutants in oil-in-water emulsions [16]. However, improper disposal of non-biodegradable polymer membrane substrates after their utilization may still cause secondary pollution to the environment [17]. Poly(lactic acid) (PLA) is recognized as a biodegradable polymer, and its biomass raw material source meets the needs of society for sustainable development [18,19]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop multifunctional PLA-based superhydrophilic membranes for wastewater treatment.

In addition to the hydrophilic modification of membrane surface, the biomimetic co-deposition technology based on plant polyphenols can also be employed to directly fabricate a selective cortex on the porous substrate under suitable conditions, thereby obtaining a multifunctional separation membrane [20,21]. Tannic acid (TA) is an inexpensive, natural, water-soluble polyphenolic compound, and it is widely used for the surface modification of materials due to its ability to interact with different substances through various binding modes, such as electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and ligand bonding [22–24]. Alkhouzaam et al. [25] prepared a highly efficient loose nanofiltration membrane on a polysulfone (PSF) substrate through the co-deposition of TA and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), demonstrating a high water permeability of up to 138 LMH/bar and excellent dye/salt selectivity. A polyphenylene sulfide (PPS) self-cleaning membrane was constructed by a double crosslinking coating on the PPS substrate, using TA-polyethyleneimine and Fe3+ as coating components [26]. The resulting membrane exhibited superhydrophilicity in air and superoleophobicity underwater, possessing excellent fouling resistance and strong light-Fenton self-cleaning capability. It should be noted that the effective combination of photocatalytic technology and surface hydrophilic modification technology can synergistically enhance the anti-pollution performance of the membrane and prolong its service life. Zhang et al. [27] fabricated a superhydrophilic multifunctional membrane by co-depositing gallic acid (GA) and tetrabutyl titanate (TBT) on the surface of a PLA electrospun membrane in an aqueous solution. The membrane demonstrated efficient separation property for a wide range of oil-water mixtures and O/W emulsions, as well as removal capability for water-soluble contaminants such as methylene blue (MB). Additionally, the UV photocatalytic property of TiO2 endows the membrane with a self-cleaning property [27], which presents a novel research idea for solving the membrane fouling problem. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, have received extensive attention in the field of photocatalysis owing to their good water solubility, wide sources, and high photostability [28–30]. A novel photocatalytic material was developed by combining xylan-based CDs with hydrogenated TiO2, which can efficiently degrade MB, Rhodamine B (RhB), and methyl orange (MO) under visible light [31]. Yu et al. [32] have elaborated on the photocatalytic mechanism of the CDs/nanomaterial system in detail and concluded that the synergistic enhancement effects of CDs mainly lie in four aspects, namely, energy band regulation, local electrical construction, active site engineering, and multi-carrier activation. Therefore, conjugation with CDs is one of the emerging strategies to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 and thus enhance the self-cleaning property of the membrane [33].

Based on the above considerations, we propose a new strategy for the surface modification of PLA porous membranes. In this method, TA, CDs, and TBT are hydrolytically co-deposited on the surface of the PLA-based membrane to form a superhydrophilic crosslinking network, thereby realizing the application of the PLA-based separation membrane in complex wastewater treatment. The resulting modified membrane exhibits superhydrophilicity in air and superoleophobicity underwater, enabling the effective separation of various O/W emulsions as well as the selective removal of cationic dyes and amphoteric dyes from wastewater. Furthermore, the modified membrane can be recycled via the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants on its surface under the irradiation of UV lamps. Therefore, this work opens up a new avenue for the design of multifunctional membranes for the treatment of complex wastewater.

The main materials include Cys-CDs/PLA (5 wt%) composite membranes, CDs, and TA, where Cys-CDs refer to L-cysteine-modified CDs. CDs were obtained by the green oxidation of weathered coal as reported in our previous work [34]. TA, TBT, Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), MB, RhB, and MO were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd., China. Sodium periodate (NaIO4, 99.5%) and Tween-80 were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. Trichloromethane (TCM), xylene, n-hexane, kerosene, oleic acid, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), and tetrachloroethylene were purchased from Tianjin Xinbote Chemical Co., Ltd., China. The chemical reagents were all of analytical purity and were used as received without further purification.

PLA-based membranes, namely Cys-CDs/PLA (5 wt%) composite membranes, were prepared using the non-solvent-induced phase separation (NIPS) method assisted by Cys-CDs. Briefly, 1.0 g PLA and 0.05 g Cys-CDs were added to 20 mL of dichloromethane and stirred magnetically until completely dissolved. Then, 5 mL of the solution was poured into a petri dish with a diameter of 6 mm, and an equal volume of C2H5OH was slowly added. Subsequently, the petri dish was placed in an environment with a relative humidity of 98% until phase separation was completed, and then it was dried under vacuum. The detailed preparation process and characterization of the PLA-based membrane are described elsewhere [35].

CDs-TiO2@TA/Cys-CDs/PLA membranes were prepared through the hydrolytic co-deposition of TA-based coatings, and the procedure is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, 2 mL of TBT/C2H5OH solution was uniformly dropped onto the surface of the superhydrophobic PLA-based membrane to wet it. Then, 10 mL of Tris-HCl solution (containing 100 mg of TA and 10 mg of CDs) was poured onto the surface of the membrane, followed by the addition of 1 mL of NaIO4 to facilitate the self-polymerization process of TA. The coating solution formulas are detailed in Table 1 . Each additive in the formulas was studied separately. After the hydrolytic co-deposition for 8 h, the synthesized membranes were thoroughly washed with deionized water and then subjected to further characterization and performance testing.

The wettability of the membrane was measured using a video optical contact angle meter (OCA 25, Data Physics, Germany), and both the dynamic water contact angle (WCA) and the static oil contact angle (UOCA) were measured. The functional groups on the membrane surface were characterized using attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet iS 20). The surface chemical states and elemental composition of the membranes were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi). The crystal structure of the membrane surface was analyzed using a SmartLab 9 kW X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD). The microstructure of the membrane was observed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI S-4800, Japan). The element distribution on the surface and cross-section of the membrane was studied through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the membrane were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method based on the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms obtained from the specific surface area analyzer (V-Sorb 2800TP). Zeta potential (Nano ZS90) analysis was performed on different formulated solutions to analyze the impact of each component of the coating on the surface charge properties of the membrane. Zeta potential measurements were conducted within a pH range of 3.0 to 11.0. The particle size distribution and morphology of the emulsion were determined using a nanoparticle potentiometer (Nano ZS90) and a digital optical microscope (XSP-BM-30 AD), respectively. The separated emulsions were extracted with tetrachloroethylene, and then the oil content was measured by an infrared oil content analyzer (JC-OIL-6, China). The dye content in the separated solutions and emulsions was determined using a UV spectrophotometer (UV 3600, Shimadzu).

The separation performance of the membrane was evaluated using a dead-end filtration device for separating a 1/100 O/W emulsion, dye (30 ppm), and a mixture of O/W emulsion and dye. The self-cleaning property of the membrane was assessed by analyzing the membrane performance after separating the oleic acid-in-water emulsion and subsequent photocatalysis. Additionally, through experiments on the adsorption behavior of the membrane toward the dyes, the principle of selective removal of cationic dyes by the membrane was analyzed.

TCM, n-hexane, xylene, kerosene, and oleic acid were selected as representative oil phases. 1 mL of each oil phase was added to 100 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 50 mg of Tween-80. The mixture was then vigorously stirred for 5 h to obtain a stable emulsion. A transmembrane pressure of 0.1 bar was applied to filter 100 mL of the emulsion. The flux and the rejection rate of the membrane were determined using formulas (1) and (2) [19].
(1)

Where ΔV (L) represents the volume of filtrate, A denotes the effective filtration area of 1.32 × 10−5 m2, Δt (h) stands for the filtration time, ΔP (bar) indicates the filtration pressure, C0 (L/L) represents the initial oil content of the emulsion, and C1 (L/L) signifies the oil content of the filtrate.

The cationic dye MB, the amphoteric dye RhB, and the anionic dye MO were selected and prepared as stock solutions with a concentration of 500 mg/L for subsequent use. More information about the dyes is provided in Table S1. The dye concentration in both the selective removal experiment and the adsorption kinetics experiment of the membrane was 30 mg/L. The calculation method for the dye removal rate was the same as that for the rejection rate of emulsion, except that the difference lay in obtaining the concentration of dye before and after separation using a UV spectrophotometer. The isothermal adsorption test and adsorption thermodynamics test of the membrane for the dyes were carried out using a series of low-concentration dye solutions prepared by diluting the stock solution. More relevant calculation information can be found in the Supporting Information.

The self-cleaning performance of the membrane was evaluated by its recycling performance after catalytic degradation of oil or dye pollutants on the surface of the membrane under UV lamp radiation. Oleic acid, with a typical long-chain hydrocarbon structure, was selected as a representative for detailed photocatalytic degradation effect experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of the crosslinking network
ce:section.ce:para: The crosslinking network was formed on the surface of PLA-based membranes during the hydrolytic co-deposition of TA, CDs and TBT, and the possible formation mechanism is depicted in Fig. 1. The reactions involved in this process were carried out on the membrane surface and could be divided into three types, as shown in detail below. Firstly, TBT rapidly hydrolyzes to form hydrophilic TiO2 upon contact with H2O [36,37]. The surface of water-soluble CDs is rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [34], and carboxyl groups can coordinate with hydrophilic TiO2 to form complexes [27,38]. Secondly, since the self-polymerization deposition process of TA is relatively slow, the addition of NaIO4 can accelerate the oxidation of the phenolic hydroxyl group of catechol in the TA molecule to the quinone group, thereby facilitating the rapid deposition of TA molecules on the PLA substrate and the formation of a stable deposition layer [39,40]. Lastly, the carbonyl oxygen in the PLA molecular chain acts as hydrogen bond acceptors, while both the hydroxyl groups in the CDs structure and those in the TA molecule serve as hydrogen bond donors. The formation of hydrogen bonds not only enhances the degree of crosslinking in the coating, but also strengthens the binding force between the coating and the substrate [20]. It can be seen from the above that the preparation method of this superhydrophilic crosslinking network has the advantages of simple operation, low energy consumption, and easy industrial application. However, the main challenge lies in the large-scale production of coal-based CDs [34]. Therefore, in-depth research into the formation mechanism and action mechanism of the superhydrophilic crosslinking network will help to find alternative raw materials for coal-based CDs, thus further promoting the practical application of this technology.

To verify the formation of the crosslinking network mentioned above, the chemical changes in coating solutions with different formulas were tracked and analyzed using a UV spectrophotometer. All solutions were diluted 100 times for testing. The aqueous solution of CDs and the ethanol solution of TBT exhibited strong UV absorption, as depicted in Fig. 2c and Fig. 2f. When these two solutions are mixed, there is a change in the absorption wavelength of the mixture, and the UV absorption intensity of the mixture increases over time. The results show that TBT undergoes a hydrolysis reaction instantly to generate TiO2 upon contact with the aqueous solution of CDs and gradually complexes with the carboxyl groups of CDs [31]. Compared with the UV characteristic absorption peaks of TA molecules in Fig. 2d, the new UV absorption peaks at 252 nm and 365 nm in Fig. 2e are ascribed to the quinone structure [40], suggesting that NaIO4 efficiently promotes the oxidation and deposition of TA molecules and is not affected by CDs (Fig. 2g) or TBT (Fig. 2h). It should be noted that when CDs and TBT were simultaneously added to the oxidation solution of TA, there was a significant change in the oxidation process of TA (Fig. 2i), indicating that a crosslinking network was formed between these components. In conclusion, CDs can undergo complexation reactions with TiO2 due to the presence of carboxyl groups on their surface. The abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CDs [34] endow the CDs-modified TiO2 (CDs-TiO2) particles with hydrophilicity. TA, with its surface-independent adhesion property, can act as a binder to stably anchor the CDs-TiO2 on the surface of the PLA-based membrane via hydrogen bonds, and hybridize with CDs-TiO2 through polyphenol hydroxyl groups on the surface of TA to form a hydrophilic crosslinking network.

Considering that the wettability of the membrane affects the separation efficiency of O/W emulsion and the anti-fouling property of the membrane [41,42], the dynamic water contact angle (WCA) and underwater heavy oil contact angle (UOCA) of modified membranes with different formulas were first evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3a, only M-1 exhibited hydrophobicity, indicating that the CDs-TiO2 failed to effectively improve the wettability of the PLA-based membrane. This might be due to the weak binding force between the CDs-TiO2 and the PLA-based membrane, which prevented them from attaching effectively. When TA was involved in the co-deposition, all the membranes except M-1 exhibited good hydrophilicity. However, only the M-5 membrane could be instantly wetted upon contact with water droplets (Fig. 3a, Fig. 4a, and Video 1). This can be attributed to the formation of a strong crosslinking network by the components on the surface of the basement membrane, as well as a large number of hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on its surface masking the hydrophobicity of ester groups on the surface of PLA-based membranes (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3b demonstrates that only the M-5 membrane exhibits superoleophobicity, featuring an UOCA of 154°, while the remaining membranes show ordinary oleophobicity, indicating that the M-5 membrane can rapidly form a hydrated layer on its surface underwater to isolate oil. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that the contact angles of the M-5 membrane with various organic solvents underwater are all >150°, indicating that the M-5 membrane also exhibits superoleophobicity in this case, which provides a solid foundation for the subsequent separation of different O/W emulsions. Based on the above results, the following research work mainly focuses on the M-5 membrane. The charged properties on the membrane surface affect the selectivity of the membrane for pollutant separation [43]. As shown in Fig. 3e, among the components involved in the surface composition of the M-5 membrane, TBT has a small positive charge, while other components and all formulas are negatively charged due to their abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The M-5 membrane is negatively charged at different pH values, and it carries the most negative charge in the pH range of 6–9 (Fig. 3f). Therefore, it can be predicted that the M-5 membrane will selectively capture positively charged pollutants in wastewater.

Considering that the wettability of the membrane affects the separation efficiency of O/W emulsion and the anti-fouling property of the membrane [41,42], the dynamic water contact angle (WCA) and underwater heavy oil contact angle (UOCA) of modified membranes with different formulas were first evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3a, only M-1 exhibited hydrophobicity, indicating that the CDs-TiO2 failed to effectively improve the wettability of the PLA-based membrane. This might be due to the weak binding force between the CDs-TiO2 and the PLA-based membrane, which prevented them from attaching effectively. When TA was involved in the co-deposition, all the membranes except M-1 exhibited good hydrophilicity. However, only the M-5 membrane could be instantly wetted upon contact with water droplets (Fig. 3a, Fig. 4a, and Video 1). This can be attributed to the formation of a strong crosslinking network by the components on the surface of the basement membrane, as well as a large number of hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on its surface masking the hydrophobicity of ester groups on the surface of PLA-based membranes (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3b demonstrates that only the M-5 membrane exhibits superoleophobicity, featuring an UOCA of 154°, while the remaining membranes show ordinary oleophobicity, indicating that the M-5 membrane can rapidly form a hydrated layer on its surface underwater to isolate oil. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that the contact angles of the M-5 membrane with various organic solvents underwater are all >150°, indicating that the M-5 membrane also exhibits superoleophobicity in this case, which provides a solid foundation for the subsequent separation of different O/W emulsions. Based on the above results, the following research work mainly focuses on the M-5 membrane. The charged properties on the membrane surface affect the selectivity of the membrane for pollutant separation [43]. As shown in Fig. 3e, among the components involved in the surface composition of the M-5 membrane, TBT has a small positive charge, while other components and all formulas are negatively charged due to their abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The M-5 membrane is negatively charged at different pH values, and it carries the most negative charge in the pH range of 6–9 (Fig. 3f). Therefore, it can be predicted that the M-5 membrane will selectively capture positively charged pollutants in wastewater.

The stability of oil-water separation is affected by the anti-adhesion performance of the membrane. To evaluate the anti-oil droplet adhesion performance of the M-5 membrane, the underwater oil dynamic sliding method was employed [15,23]. In simple terms, the oil droplet was dropped onto the membrane surface and then pulled to slide by the needle. As illustrated in Fig. 4b and c, both light and heavy oils can be effortlessly separated from the membrane surface without any adhesion during this process. This suggests that the M-5 membrane exhibits excellent underwater anti-oil adhesion performance, which is also clearly reflected in Videos 2 to 4.

The stability of oil-water separation is affected by the anti-adhesion performance of the membrane. To evaluate the anti-oil droplet adhesion performance of the M-5 membrane, the underwater oil dynamic sliding method was employed [15,23]. In simple terms, the oil droplet was dropped onto the membrane surface and then pulled to slide by the needle. As illustrated in Fig. 4b and c, both light and heavy oils can be effortlessly separated from the membrane surface without any adhesion during this process. This suggests that the M-5 membrane exhibits excellent underwater anti-oil adhesion performance, which is also clearly reflected in Videos 2 to 4.

The changes in surface composition before and after membrane modification were analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 5a, compared with the M-0 membrane, the new peak at 457.36 eV in the spectrum of the M-5 membrane is attributed to the Ti2p [20,44], indicating hydrolysis of TBT to TiO2 and its successful immobilization on the membrane surface. Fig. 5b-e demonstrates the high-resolution XPS C1s and O1s spectra of the M-0 and M-5 membranes. It can be seen that the CO peak intensity in M-5 membrane is significantly lower than that in M-0 membrane, and the peak located at 530.00 eV is assigned to the TiO bond in the high-resolution XPS O1s spectrum of the M-5 membrane. These results further confirm that the hydrophobic ester group on the surface of the M-5 membrane is reduced, and TBT is hydrolyzed to TiO2 and complexation occurs. The XRD spectra of M-0 and M-5 membranes are shown in Fig. 5f. The characteristic diffraction peaks were observed from the PLA-based membranes at 2θ = 12.5°, 15.1°, 16.8°, 19.0°, and 22.5°, corresponding to the α-phase crystalline structure [45], which indicates that the M-0 membrane has good crystallinity on its surface. After the formation of a hydrophilic crosslinking network on the surface, in addition to the above characteristic diffraction peaks, a new diffraction peak was detected at 2θ = 28.3°, which denotes the (210) or (002) crystal plane of TiO2 [46]. This is indicative that the crosslinking network formed on the surface of the M-5 membrane retains the micro-nanostructure formed by crystallization during phase separation of the basement membrane.

The micro-nanostructure of the membrane surface is another important factor affecting the formation of superhydrophilic surfaces [15,41]. The changes in the microstructure and elemental composition on the membrane surface before and after modification were observed by SEM and EDS. As shown in Fig. 6 , on the surface of the M-0 membrane, PLA molecules are highly crystallized to form a multi-level micro-nano structure composed of nanospheres and lamellar nanofibers, and the polymer skeleton is relatively smooth (Fig. 6a1 and 6a2). Following the coating process, the multi-level micro-nano structure remained unchanged, while the M-5 membrane exhibited a rough surface on the polymer skeleton, as depicted in Fig. 6b1 and 6b2. The hydrophilic CDs-TiO2 particles create sufficient surface roughness on the surface of the multi-level micro-nano structure [47], and this multi-level micro-nano rough structure, combined with the abundant hydrophilic units in the crosslinking network, synergistically contributes to the superhydrophilicity of the M-5 membrane [27]. This change is mainly observed in the surface structure of the membrane, while the internal porous structure remains largely unchanged (Fig. S2). EDX scanning (Fig. 6a3, Fig. 6b3, and Fig. S3) revealed the presence of the Ti element on the surface of M-5 membrane in comparison with M-0 membrane, further confirming that TiO2 was successfully immobilized on the surface of the PLA-based membrane. Moreover, the BET analysis results of the M-0 and M-5 membranes (Fig. S4) show that the internal pore size distribution of both has not changed, while the specific surface area of the latter has slightly increased (Fig. S4), which may be attributed to the increased roughness caused by the CDs-TiO2 particles on the surface of M-5 membrane. The increased surface roughness reduces oil adhesion force on the M-5 membrane while increasing dye adsorption capacity [48], thereby improving oil-water separation efficiency and dye removal performance of the M-5 membrane.

In summary, the crosslinking network formed by co-depositing of CDs, TiO2, and TA not only enhances the surface negative charge of the PLA-based membrane, but also enables the PLA-based membrane to form a superhydrophilic surface, endowing it with underwater superoleophobicity and good anti-oil adhesion.

To evaluate the separation performance of the M-5 membrane for emulsions, a dead-end filtration separation device was used to carry out separation operations on four O/W emulsions: TCM, n-hexane, xylene, and kerosene. The flux and rejection rate of the M-5 membrane for the four O/W emulsions are shown in Fig. 7b. It is evident that for four distinct emulsified oil-water mixtures, the flux and rejection rate of the M-5 membrane were higher than 440 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 99%, respectively. The size distribution and morphology of oil droplets before and after membrane separation were observed by particle size analysis and an optical microscope. Compared to the emulsion before separation, no obvious circles (oil droplets) were observed in the filtrate (Fig. 8a), and no oil droplets were detected in the particle size analysis. These results indicate that the M-5 superhydrophilic membrane exhibits excellent separation performance for stable O/W emulsions. Moreover, in the case of kerosene-in-water emulsion, the rejection rate of the M-5 membrane still remained above 97.81% after five cycle tests, while the emulsion flux decreased to 314.24 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, possibly as a result of membrane pore blockage.

To assess the mechanical stability of the M-5 membrane, a 5-cycle sonication treatment was performed. As depicted in Fig. S5, the flux and rejection rate of the M-5 membrane for the kerosene-in-water emulsion remained stable at 431 ± 11 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 98.60 ± 0.16%, respectively, throughout the test. These results demonstrate that the CDs-TiO2@TA coating, that is, the hydrophilic crosslinking network, was hardly destructed by physical stress. In addition, the chemical stability of the M-5 membrane was evaluated by exposing it to harsh pH solutions (pH = 1, 7, and 14). As illustrated in Fig. S6, in a strongly alkaline condition with a pH of 14, the M-5 membrane underwent hydrolysis and broke into fragments within 30 min. In contrast, under acidic and neutral conditions, the appearance and mass of the M-5 membrane remained virtually unchanged over a two-week experimental period, with WCA measurements confirming that the membranes retained their good hydrophilicity. This indicates that the M-5 membrane is not suitable for use under alkaline conditions, potentially limiting its practical application scope.

The negative charge on the surface of the M-5 membrane determines its adsorption performance for water-soluble pollutants with positive charges [20]. To validate the capability of the M-5 membrane in removing positively charged pollutants, three different types of ionic dyes, namely MB, RhB, and MO, were selected for evaluation. As shown in Fig. 8a, the photographs of the three dyes and their mixed solutions (all at a concentration of 30 mg/L) before and after filtration with the M-5 membrane were compared. It was found that the M-5 membrane only had good removal efficiency for positively charged cationic dyes, such as MB and RhB, and the resulting filtrates were colorless and transparent solutions. Moreover, the dye removed by the membrane is not easily diffused from the membrane surface into water, indicating that there is a strong interaction force between the dye and the membrane surface. This interaction force mainly comes from two aspects: one is the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged membrane surface and the positively charged dye [20,49], and the other is the hydrogen bonding interactions between the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the crosslinking network of the membrane surface and the dye [50]. The UV spectra of mixed dyes before and after separation in Fig. 8b also indicated that the M-5 membrane had the capability to remove positively charged dyes. As depicted in Fig. 8c, the removal rate and flux of the M-5 membrane are 99.93% and 517 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 for MB, and 99.94% and 553 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 for RhB, respectively. However, the removal rate for MO is only 42.27%. After 5 cycles, the removal rate of the M-5 membrane for MB and RhB could still remain above 99% (Fig. 8d), but the flux decreased significantly due to the strong binding force between the membrane surface and the dye molecules, making it difficult to desorb from the membrane surface [11]. Similarly, kerosene was added to the aqueous solutions containing MB or RhB dyes in order to prepare oil-in-water emulsions with the dye. A transparent and colorless solution could still be obtained after filtering the emulsions through the M-5 membrane (Fig. S7), suggesting that the M-5 membrane is capable of simultaneously separating oil droplets and positively charged organic pollutants in wastewater.

To further investigate the mechanism of the M-5 membrane for removing positively charged organic pollutants, the adsorption behaviors of M-5 membrane for MB and RhB were measured. Fig. 9a depicts the variation of the adsorption capacity of the M-5 membrane for two dyes with initial concentrations of 30 mg/L over time. Due to the relatively high concentration of dyes in the initial adsorption stage and the large number of adsorption sites on the membrane surface [51], the adsorption capacity (q
t ) of the M-5 membrane for both MB and RhB increased sharply within a short period of time. The difference lies in the fact that the adsorption equilibrium time of the M-5 membrane for MB is much shorter than that for RhB. This might be attributed to the higher positive charge of MB in aqueous solution compared to that of RhB, resulting in a stronger electrostatic interaction between MB and the membrane surface than between RhB and the membrane surface [52]. Fig. 9b and Fig. 9c present the linear fittings of pseudo-first-order [53] and pseudo-second-order [54] kinetics for the adsorption process of the two dyes by the M-5 membrane, and the fitting results are shown in Table 2 . For more detailed information on the two kinetic models, please refer to the Supporting Information. The correlation coefficients (R2) derived from the pseudo-second-order kinetic model for the two dyes (0.9990 for MB and 0.9968 for RhB) were much higher than those obtained from the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (0.6425 for MB and 0.9017 for RhB), indicating that the adsorption of MB and RhB onto the M-5 membrane fits the pseudo-second-order kinetic model better. The above results also suggest that chemical adsorption plays a dominant role in the removal process of MB and RhB. In other words, there is electrostatic attraction and π-π interaction between the M-5 membrane and MB or RhB molecules [50]. Fig. 9d illustrates the correlation between the equilibrium adsorption capacity (q
e ) and the dye concentration, confirming that an increase in dye concentration is conducive to a higher q
e . Also, the adsorption processes of MB and RhB on the M-5 membrane were further simulated using the classical Langmuir [55] and Freundlich [56] isothermal adsorption models. More detailed information about these two isothermal adsorption models can be found in the Supporting Information. The linear fittings of the Langmuir and Freundlich isothermal adsorption models for the two dyes by the M-5 membrane are shown in Fig. 9e and Fig. 9f, and the related parameters are listed in Table 3 . It is evident that the correlation coefficients (R2) of the Freundlich model for the two dyes (0.9979 for MB and 0.9973 for RhB) are higher than those obtained from the Langmuir model (0.8537 for MB and 0.9096 for RhB), indicating that the Freundlich model is more consistent with the adsorption process of MB and RhB by the M-5 membrane through multilayer adsorption [57,58]. This further confirms that the M-5 membrane is a promising O/W separation membrane capable of adsorbing and removing positively charged pollutants from water.

Thermodynamic investigations can determine the direction and extent of a physicochemical process [59]. Based on van't Hoff and Gibbs free energy law described in ref. [4], the Gibbs free energy (ΔGθ, kJ/mol), the enthalpy (ΔHθ, kJ/mol), and the entropy (ΔSθ, J·mol−1·K−1) were calculated. The van't Hoff equation is plotted in Fig. S8, and the results obtained are summarized in Table 4 . As can be seen, the values of ΔGθ for MB and RhB are both negative, indicating the spontaneity and feasibility of the adsorption process. The negative value of ΔSθ suggests a decrease in the disorder of the solid-liquid contact surface during the adsorption process [4]. The negative value of ΔHθ indicates that the adsorption of MB and RhB on the M-5 membrane is exothermic. It can also be observed that the absolute values of ΔGθ for MB and RhB are both small and change little with temperature. Therefore, the impact of temperature on adsorption in the studied process is minor, making it favorable for the application of the M-5 membrane at room temperature [7].

Based on the above analysis, the M-5 membrane can spontaneously adsorb cationic dyes MB and RhB in aqueous solution, and the adsorption process conforms to the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the Freundlich isothermal adsorption model. This adsorption process demonstrates typical multi-layer adsorption characteristics, and a reasonable mechanism for the selective removal of cationic dyes from aqueous solutions is proposed. From the above Zeta potential analysis, it can be seen that the surface of M-5 membrane is electronegative in the pH range of 3 to 11. Therefore, when the solution containing MB or RhB comes into contact with the surface of the M-5 membrane, the negatively charged M-5 membrane exhibits an adsorption effect on the positively charged MB or RhB through electrostatic interaction [60,61], and this electrostatic interaction is the main reason for the selective adsorption of cationic dyes by the M-5 membrane [62]. Once MB or RhB is captured on the surface of M-5 membrane [63], the abundant -OH and -COOH groups in the crosslinking network on its surface interact with the nitrogen-containing or oxygen-containing groups in the cationic dyes of MB or RhB to form hydrogen bonds [48,64], resulting in their being firmly adsorbed on the membrane surface and not easily diffusing from the membrane surface into water. At the same time, the condensed aromatic rings in the crosslinking network on the membrane surface can form π-π interactions with the aromatic rings in the MB or RhB molecules [65], thereby further enhancing the adsorption strength of MB or RhB on the membrane surface. In conclusion, it is precisely because of the formation of electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonds and π-π interaction between the surface of M-5 membrane and cationic dyes that M-5 membrane can effectively adsorb MB or RhB [66,67], thereby achieving the selective removal of cationic dyes in aqueous solutions. Furthermore, this work demonstrates that coating a superhydrophilic crosslinking network on the surface of PLA-based membranes is an efficient method for preparing separation membranes used to remove cationic dyes from aqueous solutions. However, considering that positively charged heavy metal pollutants are also one of the main challenges in sewage purification, future research will focus on exploring the potential application of the membrane in removing heavy metal cations from water.

Although the M-5 membrane can effectively separate oil-in-water emulsions and simultaneously achieve the selective removal of positively charged organic dyes, the contamination of the superhydrophilic membrane material by high-viscosity oil and organic dyes remains a challenging and inevitable problem. The most obvious example is that in the tests for the separation of oil-in-water emulsions or the removal of positively charged dyes, the flux of the M-5 membrane declined conspicuously after 5 cycles. The realization of self-cleaning of the membrane through the catalytic degradation of contaminants by TiO2 under UV irradiation is an effective way to maintain the efficient oil/water separation performance of the membrane [10]. It can be seen from the previous literature that the complexation of CDs with TiO2 can improve its UV absorption capacity, and the previous analyses also confirm that the complexation of CDs with TiO2 has indeed occurred in the crosslinking network on the surface of the M-5 membrane. To investigate whether the crosslinking network on the surface of the M-5 membrane possesses a self-cleaning function, the surface composition and properties of M-5 membrane, which was attached to an oleic acid-in-water emulsion as the model, were analyzed under UV irradiation for different durations. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 10a reveal that the M-5 membrane with oleic acid attached exhibits new characteristic peaks at 2925 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1, corresponding to -CH3 and -CH2 stretching vibrations, respectively [27]. With the extension of UV irradiation time, the intensity of the two new characteristic peaks on the oleic acid-contaminated M-5 membrane gradually decreased to disappear, accompanied by a significant decrease in the ester group characteristic peak at 1710 cm−1. The oleic acid-contaminated M-5 membrane had a WCA of 75o and lost its superhydrophilicity, while the M-5 membrane recovered superhydrophilicity after UV irradiation for 2 h with a WCA of 12o (Fig. 10b). These results indicate that the oleic acid on the surface of the contaminated M-5 membrane was catalytically decomposed when exposed to UV irradiation. The possible mechanism is shown in Fig. 11 . Under UV irradiation, electrons (e−) undergo excitation from the valence band of TiO2 to the conduction band, and electron holes (h+) are generated in the valence band. The photoexcited electrons in the conduction band react with atmospheric O2 to form superoxide radicals (·O2 −). Simultaneously, the holes in the valence band can oxidize surface-adsorbed water, producing hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [68]. The reactive oxygen species, including ·O2 − and ·OH, which have strong redox activity, can effectively degrade the organic pollutants into H2O, CO2, and other small molecules. In addition, CDs can transfer electrons from themselves to the conduction band of TiO2, thereby providing more participating electrons for the photocatalytic reaction while inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs, which significantly enhances the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 [10]. This synergistic photocatalytic effect between CDs and TiO₂ not only endows the M-5 membrane with excellent self-cleaning performance but also enhances its potential for recycling. However, it cannot be ruled out that the PLA molecular chains in the base membrane may be attacked by free radicals and undergo degradation under photocatalytic reaction conditions [69]. Subsequently, the M-5 membrane was further applied to continuous separation experiments of oleic acid-in-water emulsions, and self-cleaning was achieved through UV lamp irradiation after each cycle to verify its self-cleaning ability. As shown in Fig. 10c, except for the 5th cycle, the flux and the rejection rate during the repeated separation cycles are comparable to those of the 1st cycle, demonstrating that the self-cleaning property of the M-5 membrane effectively extends its service life and reduces costs. Table 5 summarizes recent representative work on multifunctional membranes in complex wastewater purification. The M-5 membrane exhibits comparable separation efficiency to other multifunctional membranes, while also possessing photocatalytic self-cleaning and degradability properties.

In summary, a novel multifunctional membrane CDs-TiO2@TA/Cys-CDs/PLA was fabricated based on the biomimetic co-deposition of TiO2 (hydrolyzed product of TBT) assisted by TA and CDs onto the hydrophobic PLA-based membrane in this study. The UV tracking analysis of solutions with different formulas indicated that TA, CDs, and TiO2 were successfully deposited on the surface of the PLA matrix and formed a crosslinking network. The measurement of physical and chemical properties showed that the surface of the superhydrophilic biomimetic membrane possesses multifunctional characteristics, including superhydrophilicity and oleophobicity, electronegativity, and excellent anti-adhesion performance. Further application results demonstrated that the superhydrophilic biomimetic membrane exhibited excellent separation performance for oil-in-water emulsions, soluble organic dye solutions, and complex wastewater containing both emulsions and dyes. Specifically, the rejection rate of the CDs-TiO2@TA/Cys-CDs/PLA membrane for various oil-in-water emulsions exceeds 99%. The CDs-TiO2@TA/Cys-CDs/PLA membrane can also selectively remove soluble dyes carrying positive charges in water through electrostatic adsorption, with a removal rate higher than 99%. Moreover, the CDs-TiO2@TA/Cys-CDs/PLA membrane was found to be easily regenerated under UV irradiation and reused effectively for five successive cycles, which proves its self-cleaning property. As a result, the multifunctional CDs-TiO2@TA/Cys-CDs/PLA membranes could have broad prospects for application in the purification of complex wastewater.
